column4: maxLat) is the ERA5 area of interest in degrees;

column1: # useMesh=1 enables the lateral ocean mesh run (open-water routes only).
# AUTO bbox: set minLon (col 9) to "auto" (the other 3 are then ignored) and the
# driver derives the AOI from the great-circle path + the mesh half-width
# (computeAoi): it samples the GC apex
# halfWidthKm + 4 deg margin. This guarantees the fetched ERA5 grid covers the
# whole route INCLUDING the high-latitude apex and the mesh's lateral detour
# room. Hand-tuned endpoint-sized bboxes clipped the apex on ocean-crossings
# (the transatlantic GC bows to ~64N but a 56N bbox ceiling dropped the north
# third out-of-grid -> NaN -> silent degrade to calm)
# use "auto". computeAoi emits a WRAP (minLon > maxLon) bbox automatically when
# the GC crosses the dateline.
# ANTIMERIDIAN: a row whose minLon > maxLon signals a bbox that WRAPS across
# the dateline (e.g. 133 .. -113 means 133E eastward to 113W). era5_fetch.py
# fetches both halves and merges onto a 0..360 axis (GDALMetoceanField samples
# it natively). Used by Yokohama->LongBeach (transpacific); auto-derived here.
# Full 12-route set
# spanning every major basin and coastal -> ocean-crossing length classes.
# The 4 ocean-CROSSINGS (rows 9-12) use mesh-ON with COARSER params (cols 14
# meshSpacingKm=80
column2: meshHalfWidthKm=1000): the §17 convergence study shows 80 km

column1: # spacing converges to within 0.006 % of 40 km

# short to deviate) and Brisbane->Newcastle (mesh route is WORSE: offshore = rougher
# Tasman Sea) gain nothing -> kept mesh-OFF.
